%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Internal (Asymptotic) Stability

> **Section Objective:** Decide internal stability of a discrete-time LTI system by looking at its characteristic roots.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Characteristic root
- Characteristic mode
- Unit circle
- Asymptotic stability
- Marginal stability
- Repeated root trap

## 1. Characteristic roots as points in the complex plane

Every characteristic root \(\gamma\) is a point in the complex plane. Writing it in polar form reveals two pieces of information: \(|\gamma|\) is the **distance from the origin** (the radius), and \(\beta\) is the **angle** from the positive real axis.

These two numbers tell you everything about how the corresponding mode behaves.

**Minimal example:**
- \(|\gamma| = 0.8\) — the root sits inside the unit circle.
- \(|\gamma| = 1.2\) — the root sits outside the unit circle.

### EXAM TRIGGER

Whenever a problem gives characteristic roots, immediately compare each magnitude \(|\gamma|\) with 1.

### COMMON MISUSE

Do **not** compare the real part alone with 1. A root like \(\gamma = 0.3 + 0.95j\) has real part \(0.3\) but magnitude \(\sqrt{0.3^2 + 0.95^2} \approx 1.0\). Always compute \(|\gamma|\).

$$\gamma = |\gamma|e^{j\beta}$$

## 2. The internal stability test

The zero-input response of a discrete-time LTI system is built from **characteristic modes** of the form \(\gamma^n\). This formula shows the key split:

- The factor \(e^{j\beta n}\) has magnitude exactly 1 for all \(n\) — it only **rotates** the mode in the complex plane.
- The factor \(|\gamma|^n\) controls the **size** of the mode.

**Three outcomes based on \(|\gamma|\):**

| \(|\gamma|\) | Behavior of \(|\gamma|^n\) |
|---|---|
| \(|\gamma| < 1\) | Decays to 0 |
| \(|\gamma| > 1\) | Grows without bound |
| \(|\gamma| = 1\) | Stays constant (unless repeated-root factors appear) |

### NEAR-MISS TO AVOID

A complex root may produce oscillation because \(\beta \neq 0\). But **oscillation is not instability**. The mode is still stable as long as \(|\gamma| < 1\). Oscillation only becomes a problem if the magnitude grows or the repeated-root trap applies.

$$\gamma^n = |\gamma|^n e^{j\beta n}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%


## 3. Boundary cases: marginal stability versus instability

A discrete-time LTI system is **asymptotically stable** if and only if every characteristic root \(\gamma_k\) lies **strictly inside** the unit circle — that is, every root satisfies \(|\gamma_k| < 1\).

Here \(\gamma_k\) denotes the \(k\)-th characteristic root of the system's characteristic equation.

**When to apply this test:** After forming or receiving the characteristic equation of the difference equation, find all roots and check each magnitude.

**Minimal example:**
- Roots \(0.5\) and \(-0.7\): both have magnitude less than 1. ✓ Asymptotically stable.
- Roots \(0.5\) and \(1.1\): the second root has \(|1.1| = 1.1 > 1\). ✗ Unstable.

### EXAM TRIGGER

Whenever roots are listed or a pole/root plot is shown, check every single root against the unit circle.

### COMMON MISUSE

Saying "most roots are inside" is not sufficient. **One root outside the unit circle makes the entire system unstable.** The condition must hold for every \(\gamma_k\) without exception.

$$|\gamma_k| < 1 \quad \text{for every characteristic root } \gamma_k$$

## 3. Boundary cases: marginal stability versus instability

Roots exactly on the unit circle (\(|\gamma| = 1\)) require special care — they are neither clearly stable nor clearly unstable without further inspection.

**Case 1 — Simple (unrepeated) root on the unit circle:**
If no roots lie outside the unit circle and some roots lie exactly on the unit circle but are **not repeated**, the system is **marginally stable**. The corresponding modes have constant magnitude and do not decay, but they also do not grow.

**Case 2 — Repeated root on the unit circle:**
If a root on the unit circle has multiplicity \(r > 1\), the zero-input response includes the factor \(n^{r-1}\gamma^n\). When \(|\gamma| = 1\) and \(r > 1\), this factor **grows without bound** as \(n \to \infty\), making the system **unstable**.

Here \(r\) is the **multiplicity** of the repeated root.

**Minimal example:**
- Single root at \(e^{j\pi/3}\): \(|\gamma| = 1\), simple — **marginally stable**.
- Double root at \(e^{j\pi/3}\): \(|\gamma| = 1\), \(r = 2\), mode includes \(n \cdot e^{j\pi n/3}\) — **unstable**.

### COMMON MISUSE

Repeated roots **inside** the unit circle are not the problem. Even with \(r > 1\), if \(|\gamma| < 1\), then \(|\gamma|^n \to 0\) fast enough to dominate the polynomial factor \(n^{r-1}\). The dangerous case is repeated roots **on** the unit circle only.

$$n^{r-1}\gamma^n$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- **Polar form of a characteristic root:** \(\gamma = |\gamma|e^{j\beta}\), where \(|\gamma|\) is the distance from the origin and \(\beta\) is the angle.
- **Characteristic mode magnitude:** \(\gamma^n = |\gamma|^n e^{j\beta n}\). The angle term only rotates; the size is controlled entirely by \(|\gamma|^n\).
- **Asymptotic stability condition:** \(|\gamma_k| < 1\) for every characteristic root \(\gamma_k\). All roots must be strictly inside the unit circle — one outside root is enough to fail.
- **Repeated-root boundary mode:** \(n^{r-1}\gamma^n\). When \(|\gamma| = 1\) and \(r > 1\), this grows without bound.
- **Classification rules:**
  - All roots strictly inside unit circle → **asymptotically stable**
  - Any root outside unit circle → **unstable**
  - No roots outside, some simple unrepeated roots on the unit circle → **marginally stable**
  - Any repeated root on the unit circle → **unstable**
- **Key trap:** Repeated roots inside the unit circle are safe. Repeated roots on the unit circle are unstable. Location matters, not repetition alone.

*Next, use this root-location test whenever a difference equation or pole plot asks for discrete-time system stability.*
